title: Buddhism and Shintoism in Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Buddhism and Shintoism in Evangelion             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Fri Aug 08, 2008 9:07 am Well, some may have noticed, but aside from Judeo-Christian imageries and symbols, there is the metaphysical part which doesn't fit into the Judeo-Christian category. I will type out the whole of my observations to a later date, so it would be really nice if I get some ideas. Learning is a very good thing for me...

[]:             Quote Post by Action_Bastard » Mon Aug 11, 2008 11:33 pm Well, Shintoism is harmony with nature, if I am not mistaken. So, the fact that the Geofront if quite a nice looking place with a lake, grassy fields, and lush forests makes me think that there has to be some Shinto going on in there.

[]:             Quote Post by Noephon » Mon Aug 11, 2008 11:42 pm There's more to Shinto than that. Harmony with nature is important but it's just an outgrowth of the idea in Shinto that everything proceeds from the Great Original Kami. I'd be interested in reading The Imperialist's thoughts in full. I read Yamakage's Essence of Shinto a while back but it's still somewhat confusing to me and as strange as this may sound seeing it couched in Eva terms may clear up my confusion. At least somewhat...

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Aug 13, 2008 8:47 am Alright, I decided to advance the arguemtns little by little, instead of one whopping big essay, so here it goes (Because Shintoism and Buddhism has co-existed for over a millenium, I will keep thing together) The part about death, in Shinto rings quite a lot of bells. In Shinto, we believe that after we die, we become kami, or deities, and join with the rest (great emphasis on join) of the humans who have passed away before, in something representing Elysium. The biggest thing is that no matter if you are bad or good, you end up in the same place, as part of the greater system . There is a similarity to the Buddhist idea, that if you break away from the karmic cycle, you shall enter a realm of utter calm, utter peacefulness, which we describe as 'mu', or nothingness. Everything comes together, and resolves itself to nothingness, which could be seen, obviously in the instrumentality process. Japanese mythology (part of Shinto), with the concept of everything, was not created, but was 'begotten' from two gods, will obviously remind us of two things in NGE, and the fact that (slight difference), but the goddess that was begotten, is both the deity for creation, and destructioin. The Three Sacred Treasers, or the Imperial Regalia, seems to also mirror the components necessary for Instrumentality. More to come...[/u]

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Aug 13, 2008 10:49 am The part about death, in Shinto rings quite a lot of bells. In Shinto, we believe that after we die, we become kami, or deities, and join with the rest (great emphasis on join) of the humans who have passed away before, in something representing Elysium. The biggest thing is that no matter if you are bad or good, you end up in the same place, as part of the greater system .

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Wed Aug 13, 2008 11:29 am Well, technically, everybody becomes a kami. Even if you are not accomplished, you become a deity of the family. You only become one of those specific deities, like as you said, when you do something rather interesting. But that interesting things could be bad deeds. In Shinto, you are not rewarded or punished. You just go on for eternity. Well, you can't really abuse Christianity when you talk about two Gods, and I specifically mention begotten. Not born, not created, but begotten. The 'mu' idea is pretty much the main concept of Buddhism (and susquently applied to some parts of Shintoism), and so it is slightly hard to discard the 'mu' idea being taken in.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Aug 13, 2008 9:36 pm Well, technically, everybody becomes a kami. Even if you are not accomplished, you become a deity of the family. You only become one of those specific deities, like as you said, when you do something rather interesting. But that interesting things could be bad deeds. In Shinto, you are not rewarded or punished. You just go on for eternity.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Thu Aug 14, 2008 9:17 am Not all sects, but a lot of them. (may not be an understatement if we say most of them). My, I am happy that we have another comrade in this forum.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Thu Aug 14, 2008 2:30 pm I wouldn't say most. Really, the only sects that do are those which are heavily influenced by Confuscianism. In "purist" Shinto, people just chill out in Yomi forever.

[]:             Quote Post by The Imperialist » Fri Aug 15, 2008 5:11 am Hmmm, but even if, we follow that idea, it still represents how everybody will end up in the same place.
